nativeRange.habitat: Slow to moderate-flowing rivers, streams, floodplains, and paddy fields; silty or sandy substrates with leaf litter and submerged woody debris; highly vegetated margins and muddy river banks

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (essential — prevents barbel damage)
large caves and PVC pipe hides sized to fit adult body
driftwood tangles and root structures for cover
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce light intensity
dense marginal planting as visual barriers
tightly fitting lid with all gaps sealed (high escape risk)

diet.notes: Opportunistic piscivore and invertebrate feeder in the wild. In captivity, accepts earthworms, bloodworms, prawns, mussel, lance fish, whitebait, and small pieces of meaty seafood. Will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow whole — a genuine predation risk, not merely aggressive. Weaning onto dead/frozen foods is usually achievable but may require weeks of patient target-feeding at night. Pellets are occasionally accepted by settled specimens. Live earthworms and nightcrawlers are highly effective for conditioning. Feed after lights-out to match nocturnal activity.

goodWith: large cichlids (Oscar, Green Terror, Jack Dempsey) in spacious tanks
large barbs (tinfoil barbs, larger Puntius spp.)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (under ~12 cm as a rough guide)
other spiny eels of similar or smaller size (territorial conflict)
slow-moving or bottom-dwelling fish competing for the same space
fish requiring pristine hard alkaline water

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Mastacembelus armatus; shows adult coloration and characteristic tire-track pattern clearly.

summary: One of the largest freshwater spiny eels in the hobby, reaching 60–90 cm in captivity. Nocturnal ambush predator requiring a 500 L+ tank with deep fine sand, sealed lid, and large robust tankmates only. Often confused with the smaller Zig-Zag Eel (M. circumcinctus). Not for beginners.
careNotes:
The Tire Track Eel is among the most impressive freshwater eels available in the hobby and one of the most commonly misrepresented at point of sale — juveniles are often sold alongside the much smaller Zig-Zag Eel (Mastacembelus circumcinctus), and many buyers are unaware of the true adult size potential of 60–90 cm. A minimum footprint of 180 cm length and 500 L is required to house a single adult comfortably; larger is always better.

Substrate is non-negotiable: fine, smooth sand at least 8 cm deep is essential. Tire Track Eels burrow and rest partially buried; coarse gravel or sharp substrate will abrade and permanently damage the sensory barbels around the rostral tip, leading to infection and chronic health decline. Sand also reduces stress by allowing the fish to exhibit natural burrowing behaviour.

The lid must be completely sealed. Mastacembelus armatus is notorious for finding and exploiting any gap — around filter inlets, heater cables, or imperfectly-seated panels. Escape attempts typically occur at night and result in the fish desiccating on the floor. Use foam weatherstripping or purpose-cut acrylic panels to seal every opening.

Lighting should be kept dim or supplemented with floating plants; a large cave or PVC pipe section sized to fit the adult body is essential and will be used as a daytime refuge. The fish is strongly nocturnal and will rarely be seen unless fed after lights-out.

Water quality must be consistently high — large predatory fish with a meaty diet produce considerable waste. Oversized filtration and regular water changes (30–50% weekly) are strongly recommended. Target soft to moderately hard, neutral to slightly acidic water (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 5–15).

Conspecific and heterospecific eel aggression is common in confined spaces; single-specimen keeping is standard practice. Any tankmate must be large enough that it cannot be swallowed. The stomach of a 70 cm specimen can accommodate a surprisingly large fish; erring on the side of caution is advised.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare and has not been reliably documented in the hobbyist literature. In the wild, spawning appears to occur among floating vegetation during monsoon season. No practical captive-breeding protocol is established; the species is not considered breedable in home aquaria.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Mastacembelus armatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 90 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness, native range across South and Southeast Asia, habitat (slow rivers, paddy fields, silty substrates), diet (worms, crustaceans, small fish), nocturnal behaviour, burrowing requirements, sand substrate necessity, and escape risk notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Mastacembelus armatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (reported to 90 cm TL), taxonomy (Mastacembelidae, Synbranchiformes), native range, temperature tolerances, and diet classification (carnivore/invertivore). Confirmed wide distribution across South and Southeast Asia including Indian subcontinent, Indochina, Malay Peninsula, and Indonesia.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Spiny Eel Care
additional.notes: Trade-perspective care guide covering spiny eel genus including Mastacembelus armatus. Confirmed sand substrate requirement, escape-proofing necessity, nocturnal feeding schedule, diet of worms/shrimp/frozen meaty foods, large minimum tank size for tire track eel, and caution regarding confusion with smaller Zig-Zag Eel (M. circumcinctus).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Spiny Eels
additional.notes: UK hobbyist magazine feature on keeping mastacembelid eels. Confirmed 500 L+ for large species like M. armatus, risk of tankmate predation, need for fine sand over gravel, tight-fitting lid requirements, and note that specimens sold as juveniles are routinely misidentified between M. armatus and M. circumcinctus at trade level.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Mastacembelid Eels in the Aquarium
additional.notes: TFH feature on spiny eels covering genus Mastacembelus. Confirmed piscivorous tendencies in M. armatus at adult size, barbel damage from coarse substrate, nocturnal lifestyle, difficulty weaning onto prepared foods, and long captive lifespan (10+ years reported for well-kept specimens). Also noted that the common name 'tire track' derives from the distinctive repeating dorsal saddle-and-lateral-spot pattern resembling tyre tread impressions.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding has been documented. Breeding is considered impractical in home aquaria; all trade specimens are wild-caught or bred in large commercial tropical ponds.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 60–90 cm in aquaria; needs 500 L+ tank with deep sand substrate and tight-sealed gaps. Specialist commitment.
